texts.preview_tail: eodietary reconstruction through stable isotopes as a tool for understanding past human behavior and adaptation to environmental and social challenges. It highlights the potential of interdisciplinary
texts.full_text: onmental, geographic, and/or social factors. Bone samples from 25 middle Neolithic human individuals at seven archeological sites and comparative faunal samples were analyzed, and compared with contemporary series in Mediterranean Europe. Carbon and nitrogen isotope ratios (δ13 C and δ15 N) of bone collagen were studied to determine the dietary patterns. Dietary habits proved to be similar between communities, apart from some interpopulational variations in subsistence strategies. Their diet was based on C3 terrestrial resources with a major vegetal protein component. The reported variations in interpopulational subsitence strategies were not related to environmental, geographic, and/or social factors, which suggests a strong homogeneity in the diet of the Neolithic communities studied. This study contributes to a better understanding of the subsistence patterns and dietary habits of Neolithic communities in north-east Iberia, filling a gap in the knowledge of the region's prehistory. The findings provide valuable insights into how these ancient communities interacted with their environment and the resources available to them, offering a glimpse into their daily lives and survival strategies. Further research could expand on this study by analyzing more sites and comparing the results with other regions in Europe, potentially shedding light on the broader patterns and differences in Neolithic subsistence strategies across the continent. Additionally, integrating other lines of evidence, such as archaeobotanical data and artifact analysis, could offer a more comprehensive picture of the subsistence practices and economic activities of these ancient communities. In conclusion, this research underscores the importance of studying paleodietary reconstruction through stable isotopes as a tool for understanding past human behavior and adaptation to environmental and social challenges. texts.preview_tail: versity as a global leader in this important field." Tacon's appointment is part of UH's ongoing effort to strengthen its expertise in key areas that are to Hawaii's economic future, including agricul
texts.full_text: growth of the aquaculture industry." Tacon brings to UH a wealth of experience in the field of aquaculture, with over 30 years of research and development in various aspects of the industry. His work has spanned from genetic improvement of fish and shellfish to the development of aquaculture practices and "I am excited to join UH and contribute to the development of a world-class aquaculture program," Tacon said. "The commitment to sustainability and its strong research base make it an ideal place to advance the science and practice of aquaculture." AQUAHANA will work closely with UH's College of Natural Sciences, School of Ocean and Earth Science and Technology (SOEST), and the Hawaii Institute of Marine Biology (HIMB) to integrate and expand aquaculture research and development efforts across the UH system. The program will also collaborate with industry government agencies, and community organizations to address the challenges and opportunities in the aquaculture sector. "We are thrilled to have Dr. Tacon join our team," said UH Vice President for Research and Innovation Vassiliki K. Papathanasiou. "His leadership and expertise will be instrumental in advancing UH's aquaculture research and development efforts, and in positioning the university as a global leader in this important field."
